options.why: A common factor of $12$ and $18$, but not the highest — $6$ also divides both.
A common factor, but $6$ is larger and still divides both numbers.
Used the lowest common multiple of $12$ and $18$ instead of their highest common factor.
A shared factor, but $9$ itself divides both $9$ and $27$.
Chose the larger number; that is the LCM here, since $9$ divides $27$.
Assumed the numbers share only $1$, missing that $9$ divides $27$ exactly.
Multiplied $8 \times 15$; that is a common multiple, not a common factor.
Took the smaller number, but $8$ does not divide $15$.
Chose $2$, but $2$ does not divide $15$, so it is not a common factor.
A common factor, but $12$ is larger and divides both $24$ and $36$.
A common factor of both, but not the highest — $12$ also divides each.
Used the lowest common multiple of $24$ and $36$ instead of the highest common factor.
A common factor, but $21$ ($=3 \times 7$) also divides both and is larger.
Stopped at the smaller common factor $3$; $21$ is the highest shared factor.
Chose $9$, which divides $63$ but not $42$, so it is not common.
